summary: 12 concrete items surfaced from HN discussion threads; history updated and filtered against the current product archive.

items.name: Zed 1.0
items.summary: Open-source editor release praised for speed, polish, and SSH workflows.
items.why_included: It was one of the most substantive product-launch threads on the front page.
items.evidence: HN praised its low memory use and remote editing while arguing over AI defaults, search UX, and extensibility.

items.name: Structured Output Benchmark
items.source_story_title: Show HN: A new benchmark for testing LLMs for deterministic outputs
items.summary: Open benchmark for deterministic structured outputs across text, image, and audio.
items.why_included: It targets a real agent failure mode: valid JSON with wrong values.
items.evidence: Commenters focused on value accuracy, modality-specific rankings, and the need to measure structured hallucinations.

items.name: Mistral Medium 3.5
items.summary: 128B model people weighed on local speed, cost, and quantization tradeoffs.
items.why_included: It triggered serious debate about what local LLMs can realistically do.
items.evidence: HN compared its Pareto efficiency, local VRAM needs, and token throughput against Sonnet and other frontier models.

items.name: code.overheid.nl
items.source_story_title: Soft launch of open-source code platform for government
items.summary: Dutch government Forgejo pilot for shared open-source code hosting.
items.why_included: Commenters treated it as a meaningful digital-sovereignty project, not a press-release toy.
items.evidence: HN praised the open-source, European, sovereign alternative to GitHub and GitLab and discussed broader dependency concerns.

items.name: RegelRecht
items.source_story_title: Soft launch of open-source code platform for government
items.summary: Machine-readable law engine that executes Dutch regulations deterministically.
items.why_included: It expands the thread from code hosting into executable public-sector logic.
items.evidence: Commenters quoted the structured YAML / decision-logic description and discussed uses like automated policy checks and transparent benefits decisions.

items.name: Tangled
items.source_story_title: We need a federation of forges
items.summary: ATProto-based forge platform aiming for self-hostable, permanent software.
items.why_included: It was the clearest concrete product in the forge-federation thread.
items.evidence: HN debated forge federation while Tangled supporters emphasized open development, social graph integration, and self-hosting.

items.name: AT Protocol
items.source_story_title: At Protocol: Building the Social Internet
items.summary: Decentralized social protocol centered on user-owned data and portable graphs.
items.why_included: Commenters kept returning to it as the architecture behind data portability and app interoperability.
items.evidence: HN compared it to email and RSS, highlighted PDS hosting, and discussed moving social graphs between apps.

items.name: OpenTrafficMap
items.summary: V2X traffic map built on cheap hardware and a modern OSM/Mapbox theme.
items.why_included: It drew unusually concrete praise for design plus low-cost technical feasibility.
items.evidence: Commenters loved the modern palette, noted the sub-£20 hardware angle, and debated coverage and docs.

items.name: Cursor Camp
items.summary: Neal.fun browser game with social, nostalgic, badge-hunt energy.
items.why_included: It was one of the most positively received playful launches on the front page.
items.evidence: HN called it adorable, Club Penguin-like, and fun, while trading clues about hidden badges and controls.

items.name: GliaX/Stethoscope
items.source_story_title: An open-source stethoscope that costs between $2.5 and $5 to produce
items.summary: Open-source stethoscope designed to cost just a few dollars to make.
items.why_included: The thread turned a simple hardware project into a serious discussion about medical utility and manufacturing.
items.evidence: Commenters compared it with Littmann scopes, argued over price versus quality, and raised sterilization and regulatory concerns.

items.name: Laws of UX
items.summary: UX reference site commenters used as a design checklist and AI review aid.
items.why_included: It surfaced as a practical reference rather than just a design poster.
items.evidence: HN commenters proposed using it to bulk-check screens with AI, though others criticized the page as shallow and overdesigned.

items.name: Agentic test harness for play-testing
items.source_story_title: Letting AI play my game – building an agentic test harness to help play-testing
items.summary: Play-testing harness for letting AI test games and surface regressions.
items.why_included: The thread gave a concrete workflow for AI-assisted QA in game development.
items.evidence: Commenters discussed headless simulation, browser snapshots, accessibility refs, and code-plus-browser tooling to catch regressions.
